levels.title: Practical Exercises and Case Studies
levels.content:
Learning Module 8 – Incident Response and Forensics – is an advanced, hands-on training module developed under NG-SOC Work Package 5. It provides an immersive, scenario-based learning experience to improve competencies in incident detection, incident response, threat intelligence utilisation, and digital forensics. The module consists of five realistic exercises targeting core cybersecurity roles (SOC Analyst, Incident Responder, CTI Analyst, Penetration Tester, and Incident Response Coordinator). Each exercise is aligned with the European Cybersecurity Skills Framework (ECSF) role profiles and mapped to Sub Case 1a of CYNET (instructor-led training). The content emphasises manual incident response processes, cross-team coordination, threat analysis, and secure recovery practices in a financial sector context, without reliance on automated response workflows. By engaging in problem-solving and decision-making under realistic conditions, learners develop critical thinking and preparedness for real-world incidents. This module supports the NG-SOC project’s objectives under WP5:M8 and will feed into piloting and feedback activities in WP6.

[Download LM8 Guide](https://studio-ngsoc-training-platform.eurodyn.com/assets/courseware/v1/870af3988751a5bcedd6449618d497b6/asset-v1:NG-SOC+LM8+2025Q1+type@asset+block/NG-SOCD5.8LearningModule8SubCase1av0.12.pdf?raw=true)

levels.title: Level 01 — Lab access and navigation
levels.cloud_content:
### Goal
Access at least **one** lab machine and learn how to open **SSH / GUI** sessions in KYPO/CRCZ.

### Connection guide (CRCZ-style)
1. Look at the **topology/diagram** (right-hand panel).
2. Find the VM you need and **right-click** it.
3. Select:
   - **GUI/RDP** for Windows workstations
   - **SSH** for Linux machines
4. A new tab/window opens — log in with the credentials below.

### Credentials
**Linux (SSH)** (try either account):
- `ubuntu` / `qwerty!23`
- `debian` / `qwerty!23`

**Windows (GUI/RDP)** (use the Credentials panel if provided):
- `windows` / `qwerty!23`

> Password note: `qwerty!23` includes `!` and is case-sensitive.

### What to submit
Type: **“OK — access confirmed”** once you have opened at least **one** machine.
levels.local_content:
Before proceeding further, you need to start a sandbox on your machine. 
Please clone [this Gitlab repository](https://<link_to_a_repo>) and follow steps in the README file.
When your sandbox is ready at your host, submit the passkey from the README file.

levels.title: Level 02 — On-host briefing (welcome note)
levels.content:
### Goal
Confirm the exercise context using an **on-host artefact** deployed by the lab.

### Do this (guided)
1. Open your **Windows workstation** (GUI/RDP).
2. Open **File Explorer**.
3. Go to: `C:\Labs`
4. Open: `WELCOME.txt`
5. In the **first line**, identify the **environment name** mentioned.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the **environment name** exactly as written (capitalisation matters).
levels.solution: Open C:\Labs\WELCOME.txt and copy the environment name from the first line. Submit: Random Education Platform.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: On the Windows workstation, go to C:\Labs and open WELCOME.txt. The first line contains the environment name you need to copy exactly.

levels.title: Level 03 — Identify relevant systems (topology)
levels.content:
### Goal
Confirm the expected network by identifying the Windows workstation’s **Default Gateway**.

### Do this (guided)
1. On the workstation, open **Command Prompt**.
2. Run:
   `ipconfig`
3. In your active adapter, find **Default Gateway**.
4. Copy the gateway IP exactly.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the Default Gateway IP.
levels.solution: Run ipconfig and submit the Default Gateway value: 10.20.20.1.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: On the Windows workstation, run ipconfig in Command Prompt and look under your active adapter for Default Gateway.

levels.title: Level 04 — Phishing triage: primary evidence
levels.content:
### Goal
Reach the phishing exercise entry point used in this lab.

### Do this (guided)
1. Open **File Explorer**.
2. Go to: `C:\Users\Public\Desktop`
3. Open: `LiveSession.url`
4. Copy the URL:
   - If it opens in a browser, copy the address bar URL.
   - If it doesn’t, right-click → **Open with** → **Notepad**, then copy the URL.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Paste the URL exactly.
levels.solution: Read the URL from LiveSession.url and submit: https://rep-core.internal/live

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: On the Windows workstation, open C:\Users\Public\Desktop\LiveSession.url. If it doesn’t open directly, open it with Notepad to see the URL.

levels.title: Level 05 — Quick content assessment (working folder)
levels.content:
### Goal
Locate the lab working folder where trainee materials are stored.

### Do this (guided)
1. Open **File Explorer**.
2. In the address bar, type: `C:\Labs`
3. Press Enter.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the folder path exactly.
levels.solution: Navigate to the working folder and submit the path: C:\Labs

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: In File Explorer, type the lab folder path into the address bar and press Enter.

levels.title: Level 06 — Endpoint triage (system state)
levels.content:
### Goal
Confirm the welcome note trainees are expected to access.

### Do this (guided)
1. In `C:\Labs`, locate the welcome note file.
2. If needed, use the search box and type: `welcome`
3. Record the filename exactly (including extension).

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the filename exactly.
levels.solution: The welcome note filename is: WELCOME.txt

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: In C:\Labs, find the welcome note file and copy the filename exactly (including the extension).

levels.title: Level 07 — System logs (evidence of execution)
levels.content:
### Goal
Identify the telemetry collector **service name** used for evidence collection.

### Do this (guided)
1. Open **PowerShell**.
2. Run:
   `Get-Service -Name REPCollector`
3. Confirm the service **Name** shown in the output.

**Fallback (if you get “not found”):**
- Run: `Get-Service | Where-Object { $_.Name -like "*REP*" }`

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the service name exactly.
levels.solution: The collector service name is: REPCollector

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: In PowerShell, check the collector service with Get-Service -Name REPCollector. If that fails, list services containing “REP”.

levels.title: Level 08 — Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) and scope
levels.content:
### Goal
Locate the collector configuration file (forwarding/ingest settings).

### Do this (guided)
1. Open **File Explorer**.
2. Go to: `C:\ProgramData\REP\`
3. If `ProgramData` is hidden: enable **View → Hidden items**.
4. Find: `collector.yaml`

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the full path exactly.
levels.solution: The full config path is: C:\ProgramData\REP\collector.yaml

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: The config is under C:\ProgramData\REP\. If you can’t see ProgramData, enable Hidden items in File Explorer.

levels.title: Level 09 — Hunt across the environment (basic)
levels.content:
### Goal
Identify where logs are forwarded via **syslog**.

### Do this (guided)
1. Open: `C:\ProgramData\REP\collector.yaml` (Notepad).
2. Ctrl+F → search for: `syslog`
3. In the syslog section, copy the `host` value.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the syslog host exactly.
levels.solution: The syslog host value is: soc-gateway.internal

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: Open collector.yaml, search for syslog, then look for the host: field in that section.

levels.title: Level 10 — Incident timeline
levels.content:
### Goal
Identify the syslog port (useful for containment/blocking decisions).

### Do this (guided)
1. In `collector.yaml`, stay in the `syslog` section.
2. Copy the `port` value.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the port number.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: Stay in the syslog section of collector.yaml and copy the port: value.

levels.title: Level 11 — Containment actions
levels.content:
### Goal
Locate the collector **log file path** (evidence for your containment note).

### Do this (guided)
1. Go to: `C:\ProgramData\REP\`
2. Find the collector log file.
3. If unsure, open `collector.yaml` and search for `log` / `logging`.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the full log path exactly.
levels.solution: The collector log path is: C:\ProgramData\REP\collector.log

hints.title: New hint
hints.content: In C:\ProgramData\REP\, look for the collector’s .log file (or search collector.yaml for where it writes logs).

levels.title: Level 12 — Eradication (remove artefacts/persistence)
levels.content:
### Goal
Identify the HTTPS ingest endpoint (used to validate recovery telemetry).

### Do this (guided)
1. Open `collector.yaml`.
2. Ctrl+F → search for: `https`
3. In the HTTPS section, copy the `url` value.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Paste the URL exactly.
levels.solution: The HTTPS ingest URL is: https://rep-core.internal/ingest

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: In collector.yaml, search for https and copy the url: value in that transport section.

levels.title: Level 13 — Recovery and validation
levels.content:
### Goal
Find the “heartbeat interval” used by the collector script (cadence for timeline entries).

### Do this (guided)
1. Open: `C:\ProgramData\REP\collector.ps1`
2. Ctrl+F → search for: `Start-Sleep`
3. Record the number after `-Seconds`.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the number of seconds.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: Open C:\ProgramData\REP\collector.ps1 and search for Start-Sleep. The interval is the number after -Seconds

levels.title: Level 14 — Internal communication (update)
levels.content:
### Goal
Identify which Windows Event Log channel is used by the collector script.

### Do this (guided)
1. In `collector.ps1`, find:
   `$eventLogName = '...'`
2. Copy the value exactly (including case).

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the value.
levels.solution: The Event Log channel is: Application

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: In collector.ps1, find $eventLogName = '...' and copy the value between quotes.

levels.title: Level 15 — Final report (short template)
levels.content:
### Goal
Identify the Event Source name (useful in final reporting).

### Do this (guided)
1. In `collector.ps1`, find:
   `$eventSource = '...'`
2. Copy the value exactly.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the value.
levels.solution: The Event Source name is: TraineeCollectorScript

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: In collector.ps1, find $eventSource = '...' and copy the value exactly.

levels.title: Level 16 — Optional advanced bonus (single instance)
levels.content:
### Goal (optional)
Identify the REP backend node used for practical labs (from the topology).

### Do this (guided)
1. Look at the topology diagram (right-hand panel).
2. Find the VM labelled for practical labs.
3. Copy its label exactly.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the VM label exactly.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: Use the topology diagram. Look for the VM labelled as the practical labs backend and copy its label exactly.

levels.title: Level 17 — Close-out and lessons learned
levels.content:
### Goal
Identify the gateway/router VM label (topology final check).

### Do this (guided)
1. In the topology diagram, locate the router/gateway node.
2. Copy its label exactly.

### Unlock answer (trainee)
Type the router label.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: In the topology diagram, locate the router/gateway node connecting the networks and copy its label exactly.
